title: Foundations: Points, Lines & Angles
blurb: Points, lines, rays, segments and planes; measuring segments and angles; angle types, angle pairs, and bisectors — geometry from absolute zero.
buildsOn: Nothing — geometry starts here. (Numbers on a line and absolute value echo Grade 6 Integers.)

lessons.title: Points, Lines & Planes
lessons.concreteComparison: Look at a city map. Each street crossing is a dot with a name — a **point**. Each straight street runs through crossing after crossing — a **line**. And the flat map sheet everything sits on — that's a **plane**. All of geometry is built out of these three ideas.
lessons.objective: Recognize and name points, lines, and planes; know that two points determine exactly one line; tell when points are collinear.
concept: A **point** is a location. It has no size at all — we draw a dot only so you can see it. Points are named with capital letters: point $A$.

A **line** is perfectly straight and extends forever in **both** directions. Through any **two** points there is **exactly one** line — name it by the points, $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$, or with a script letter like $\ell$.
A **plane** is a perfectly flat surface extending forever in every direction, like an endless tabletop. It takes **three** points (not all on one line) to pin down a plane.
Points that sit on the same line are **collinear**. Points that don't all fit on one line are **noncollinear**.
lessons.keyIdea: Point = a location; line = straight, forever, both ways; plane = flat, forever. Two points → exactly one line.

facts.title: Two points, one line
facts.explanation: Through any two points there is exactly one line.

facts.title: Three to name a plane
facts.explanation: Three noncollinear points determine a plane.

workedExamples.statement: How many different lines pass through both point $A$ and point $B$?
workedExamples.solution: Exactly one — two points determine one line. That's what makes $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$ a well-defined name.

workedExamples.statement: Points $P$, $Q$, $R$ are noncollinear. How many lines do they determine in pairs?
workedExamples.solution: Each pair gives one line: $\overleftrightarrow{PQ}$, $\overleftrightarrow{PR}$, $\overleftrightarrow{QR}$ — three pairs, so $3$ lines.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking the drawn dot IS the point.
commonMistakes.correction: A point has **no size**. The dot is just a picture of a location.

commonMistakes.text: Treating a line as if it stops at the arrows.
commonMistakes.correction: The arrowheads mean *keeps going forever* — a line has no ends.

tryIt.statement: Points $A$, $B$, $C$ all sit on one line. How many lines pass through all three?
tryIt.solution: They're collinear, so one line already carries all three — the answer is $1$.

tryIt.statement: How many noncollinear points does it take to determine a plane?
tryIt.solution: Three — two points only pin a line; a third off that line fixes the flat surface.

steps.title: The three undefined terms
beats: A **point** is a location — no size, no width. Named with a capital: point $A$.
A **line** is straight and extends forever in **both** directions.
A **plane** is a flat surface extending forever — an endless tabletop.
Everything else in geometry is built from these three.

steps.title: Two points make a line
steps.teach: Drag either point anywhere. However you move them, **exactly one** straight line passes through both — that's the first fact of geometry.

steps.title: How we write them
beats: Point: a capital letter — $A$.
Line: two points on it, arrows both ways — $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$ — or a script letter $\ell$.
Plane: three noncollinear points — plane $ABC$. (**Noncollinear** is defined two pages ahead — for now: not all in a row.)
Order doesn't matter for a line: $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$ and $\overleftrightarrow{BA}$ are the same line.

examples.prompt: How many lines pass through both $A$ and $B$?
Noncollinear points $P, Q, R$: how many lines in pairs?

problems.prompt: How many lines pass through two given points?
problems.explanation: Two points determine exactly one line.

problems.prompt: Points $A, B, C$ are collinear (all on one line). How many lines through all three?
problems.explanation: One line already carries all three.

problems.prompt: Noncollinear $A, B, C$ taken in pairs determine how many lines?

steps.title: Collinear points
beats: Points on the **same line** are **collinear**.
Any two points are always collinear — a line runs through them.
Three points might not be: a triangle's corners are **noncollinear**.
That's why three noncollinear points pin down a **plane**.

steps.title: Planes
steps.intro: Three noncollinear points fix a flat surface.

examples.prompt: How many planes contain three given noncollinear points?
steps: Two points allow many tilted planes through their line.
A third point off the line locks the tilt — exactly $1$ plane.

steps.title: Lines and planes
steps.intro: How many points pin down a line? A plane?

problems.prompt: A camera tripod needs exactly one flat position for its 3 feet. Why 3?
problems.explanation: Three noncollinear feet fix one plane — no wobble.

problems.prompt: How many points does it take to determine a line?
problems.explanation: Two points — that's the defining fact.

steps.title: The book that started it
steps.body: Around 300 BC, Euclid opened his *Elements* with exactly these ideas — points, lines, planes — and built all of geometry from them, one justified step at a time. It became one of the most reprinted books in history, and it's the reason a four-legged table wobbles but a three-legged stool never does.

steps.title: One line
steps.prompt: Through two points, how many lines can you draw?
steps.explanation: Two points determine exactly one line.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Point** = location (no size); **line** = straight, forever, both ways; **plane** = flat, forever.
Through two points there is **exactly one line**: $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$.
**Collinear** points share a line; three **noncollinear** points determine a plane.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's lock in the basics: naming, counting lines, and collinear-or-not.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: How many lines pass through points $A$ and $B$?
problems.explanation: Two points determine exactly one line.

problems.prompt: Which figure extends forever in BOTH directions?
problems.explanation: That's the definition of a line — arrowheads both ways.

problems.prompt: Three noncollinear points determine how many lines (in pairs)?
How many noncollinear points determine a plane?
Points $P, Q, R$ all lie on line $\ell$. They are —
problems.explanation: Collinear means on the same line.

problems.prompt: How many planes contain 3 noncollinear points?
problems.explanation: Exactly one — the third point locks the tilt.

steps.title: You've finished "Points, Lines & Planes"
steps.body: You've met geometry's three building blocks and its first big fact: two points, one line. Next: Segments & Rays — pieces of a line, and why direction matters.

lessons.title: Segments & Rays
lessons.concreteComparison: A road stretches out of sight in both directions — a **line**. A laser beam starts at the pointer and shoots one way forever — a **ray**. A rope tied between two posts has two definite ends — a **segment**. Same straightness, three different objects.
lessons.objective: Distinguish lines, rays, and segments; use the notations for each; understand that ray AB and ray BA are different objects.
concept: A **segment** $\overline{AB}$ is the part of a line **between** two points $A$ and $B$ — including both. $A$ and $B$ are its **endpoints**.
A **ray** $\overrightarrow{AB}$ **starts** at $A$ (its endpoint), passes through $B$, and continues forever in that one direction.
Order matters for rays: $\overrightarrow{AB}$ starts at $A$; $\overrightarrow{BA}$ starts at $B$ and runs the other way. They are **different** rays.
Two rays from the same point in exactly opposite directions are **opposite rays** — together they form a whole line.
lessons.keyIdea: Segment = between two endpoints. Ray = one endpoint, one direction, forever. The first letter of a ray is its start.

facts.title: Three notations
facts.explanation: Line, ray, segment — the mark on top tells you which.

workedExamples.statement: On a number line, $A$ is at $2$ and $B$ is at $5$. Is the point at $9$ on ray $\overrightarrow{AB}$? On ray $\overrightarrow{BA}$?
workedExamples.solution: $\overrightarrow{AB}$ starts at $2$ and runs through $5$ toward larger numbers — $9$ is on it. $\overrightarrow{BA}$ starts at $5$ and runs toward smaller numbers — $9$ is not.

workedExamples.statement: $A$ is at $1$ and $B$ at $6$. Is the point at $4$ on segment $\overline{AB}$?
workedExamples.solution: A segment holds the points **between** its endpoints — and the endpoints themselves: $1 \le 4 \le 6$, so yes.

commonMistakes.text: Treating $\overrightarrow{AB}$ and $\overrightarrow{BA}$ as the same ray.
commonMistakes.correction: The **first** letter is the endpoint. $\overrightarrow{AB}$ starts at $A$; $\overrightarrow{BA}$ starts at $B$ and points the opposite way.
A segment **stops** at its endpoints — dots, not arrows.

tryIt.statement: $A$ is at $2$, $B$ at $5$. Name a number on $\overrightarrow{BA}$ but NOT on $\overline{AB}$.
tryIt.solution: $\overrightarrow{BA}$ passes $A$ and keeps going: any number below $2$, like $-1$.

tryIt.statement: How many endpoints does each have: segment, ray, line?
tryIt.solution: Segment $2$, ray $1$, line $0$ — that's the whole difference.

steps.title: One pair of points, three objects
steps.teach: Same two points, different objects. Toggle between them and watch the **ends**: a line never stops, a ray stops on one side only, a segment stops on both.

steps.title: Segment, ray, line
beats: **Segment** $\overline{AB}$: everything between $A$ and $B$. Two endpoints.
**Ray** $\overrightarrow{AB}$: starts at $A$, through $B$, forever onward. One endpoint.
**Line** $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$: forever both ways. No endpoints.
The little symbol on top is a picture of the object.

steps.title: Ray AB is not ray BA
beats: The **first letter names the start**.
$\overrightarrow{AB}$: departs from $A$, heading through $B$.
$\overrightarrow{BA}$: departs from $B$, heading through $A$ — the other way.
Two rays from one point in opposite directions are **opposite rays**; together they make the whole line.

steps.title: Who's on the ray?
steps.intro: Put the points on a number line and check the direction.

steps: $\overrightarrow{AB}$ starts at $2$ and runs through $5$, onward to bigger numbers.
$9 > 5$, on the far side of $B$ — yes, it's on the ray.
$\overrightarrow{BA}$ starts at $5$ and runs through $2$, toward smaller numbers.
$9$ is behind the start — not on this ray.

problems.prompt: Which notation means the part between $A$ and $B$ only?
problems.explanation: The bar with no arrows — a segment, stopping at both endpoints.

problems.prompt: $A$ at $1$, $B$ at $6$: which number is on $\overline{AB}$?
problems.explanation: $4$ is between $1$ and $6$; $8$ and $0$ are outside.

problems.prompt: How many endpoints does a ray have?
problems.explanation: A ray starts somewhere — one endpoint — and never ends.

steps.title: Two rays, one line
beats: Pick a point $P$ on a line.
$P$ splits the line into two rays going opposite ways.
Those are **opposite rays** — same endpoint, opposite directions.
Together they cover the whole line.

examples.prompt: $P$ is at $0$. One ray from $P$ contains $3$; its opposite contains $-3$. Which point is on BOTH rays?
steps: One ray covers numbers $\ge 0$, the other covers numbers $\le 0$.
Only their shared endpoint $P$ is on both.

problems.prompt: $A$ at $2$, $B$ at $5$. Which number is on $\overrightarrow{BA}$ but not on $\overline{AB}$?
Are $\overrightarrow{AB}$ and $\overrightarrow{BA}$ the same ray?

steps.title: A ray you can bounce off the Moon
steps.body: Apollo astronauts left mirror arrays on the Moon. Scientists still fire laser rays at them — each beam starts at a telescope (its endpoint) and travels one direction, just like $\overrightarrow{AB}$. The round trip takes about $2.5$ seconds and measures the Moon's distance to within millimetres.

steps.title: Read the notation
steps.prompt: Which object starts at $A$ and never ends?
steps.explanation: One arrow = a ray from $A$ through $B$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Segment** $\overline{AB}$: between two endpoints. **Ray** $\overrightarrow{AB}$: starts at $A$, forever one way. **Line**: forever both ways.
The **first letter of a ray is its endpoint** — $\overrightarrow{AB} \neq \overrightarrow{BA}$.
**Opposite rays** share an endpoint and form a whole line.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's practice telling the three objects apart and putting points on the right ray.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: How many endpoints does a segment have?
Which notation means a ray starting at $B$ through $A$?
problems.explanation: One arrow, first letter $B$ = starts at $B$.

problems.prompt: $A$ at $2$, $B$ at $5$. Which number is on $\overrightarrow{AB}$?
problems.explanation: The ray runs from $2$ through $5$ toward bigger numbers — $9$ qualifies.

problems.prompt: $A$ at $1$, $B$ at $6$. Which point is on $\overline{AB}$?
Are $\overrightarrow{PQ}$ and $\overrightarrow{QP}$ the same?
problems.explanation: A ray is named start-first; these start at different points.

problems.prompt: Two opposite rays from $P$ together form —
problems.explanation: Opposite directions from one point cover the whole line.

steps.title: You've finished "Segments & Rays"
steps.body: You can now tell a line from a ray from a segment at a glance — and read the notation. Next: Measuring Segments, where segments finally get a number.

lessons.title: Measuring Segments
lessons.concreteComparison: Measure a table with a tape measure and it doesn't matter if the table starts at the $0$ mark or at the $14$ — you read both ends and subtract. Length is a **difference of readings**, and that's exactly how geometry measures segments.
lessons.objective: Use the Ruler Postulate to find segment lengths as |a − b|, and recognize congruent segments.
concept: A **postulate** is a basic rule of geometry we accept as true without proof. The first one you'll use is the **Ruler Postulate**: the points on a line can be numbered like a ruler, one number per point (its **coordinate**).
The **length** of $\overline{AB}$, written $AB$ (no bar), is the distance between the coordinates: $AB = |a - b|$.
The absolute value makes length **never negative** — reading the ends in either order gives the same answer.
Segments with **equal lengths** are **congruent**, written $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$. In a figure, matching tick marks mean congruent.
lessons.keyIdea: Number the line like a ruler; length = |difference of the end coordinates|. Equal lengths ⇒ congruent segments.

facts.title: Length formula
facts.explanation: a, b are the endpoint coordinates — order doesn't matter.

facts.title: Congruent
facts.explanation: Same length; shown with matching tick marks.

commonMistakes.text: Reading the right-hand number as the length.
commonMistakes.correction: Unless the left end sits at $0$, you must **subtract** the two readings.
Length is $|a-b|$ — the absolute value guarantees it's never negative.

tryIt.statement: $\overline{AB}$ runs from $2$ to $7$; $\overline{CD}$ from $-1$ to $4$. Congruent?

steps.title: Slide the ruler — the length holds
steps.teach: The segment never moves; only the ruler under it slides. The two readings keep changing, but their **difference** — the length — never does. That's the Ruler Postulate.

steps.title: The Ruler Postulate
beats: A **postulate** is a basic rule we accept as true without proof — geometry's starting rules.
The **Ruler Postulate**: match the points of a line with numbers, like a ruler. Each point gets a **coordinate**.
The length of $\overline{AB}$ is $AB = |a - b|$.
Any placement of the ruler gives the same length.

steps.title: Length is never negative
beats: Subtracting in the other order flips the sign: $2 - 7 = -5$.
But a length of $-5$ means nothing.
Read the ends in either order — same length.

steps.title: Finding lengths
steps.intro: Subtract the coordinates, take the absolute value.

steps.title: Congruent segments
beats: Two segments with the **same length** are **congruent**.
Write it $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$.
Figures mark congruent segments with matching **tick marks**.
Congruent is about length — position and direction don't matter.

problems.prompt: Which segment is congruent to one from $0$ to $6$?
$\overline{PQ}$ from $-4$ to $-1$: $PQ = $ ?

steps.title: Burning an inch
steps.body: Carpenters often measure from the $1$-inch mark instead of the worn metal tip of a tape measure — they call it *burning an inch* — then subtract $1$ from the reading. It works for exactly the reason you just learned: length is a difference of readings, so where the ruler starts doesn't matter.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Ruler Postulate**: points on a line get coordinates like a ruler.
**Length** $AB = |a - b|$ — never negative, order-proof.
Equal lengths ⇒ **congruent** segments ($\cong$), marked with matching ticks.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's measure: positive coordinates, negative ones, and a congruence check.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A segment from $-6$ to $-2$ has length —
Which is congruent to a segment of length $5$ from $2$ to $7$?
The ruler slides so $A$ reads $12$ instead of $4$. The length of $\overline{AB}$ —
problems.explanation: Both readings shift together; the difference is unchanged.

steps.title: You've finished "Measuring Segments"
steps.body: Segments have numbers now — and you can spot congruent ones. Next: Segment Addition & Midpoints, where parts add up to wholes.

lessons.title: Segment Addition & Midpoints
lessons.concreteComparison: You live at one end of a straight street, your friend at the other, and the bus stop is between you. Your walk to the stop plus the walk from the stop to your friend IS the whole trip — parts add to the whole. Meet exactly **halfway** and you've found the midpoint.
lessons.objective: Use the Segment Addition Postulate (AB + BC = AC when B is between A and C) to find missing lengths, and use midpoints to halve and double.
concept: If $B$ is **between** $A$ and $C$ (all three on one line), then $AB + BC = AC$ — the **Segment Addition Postulate**.
It works backwards too: a missing part is the whole minus the known part, $BC = AC - AB$.
The **midpoint** $M$ of $\overline{AB}$ is the point that splits it into **two congruent halves**: $AM = MB = \tfrac{1}{2}AB$.
On a ruler, the midpoint's coordinate is the **average** of the endpoints: $m = \tfrac{a+b}{2}$.
lessons.keyIdea: B between A and C ⇒ AB + BC = AC. Midpoint = the point making both halves equal (coordinate = average).

facts.title: Segment addition
facts.explanation: When B is between A and C.

facts.title: Midpoint coordinate
facts.explanation: The average of the endpoint coordinates.

workedExamples.statement: $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{PQ}$ and $PQ = 14$. Find $PM$.

commonMistakes.text: Adding when a part is missing.
commonMistakes.correction: If you know the whole $AC$ and one part, **subtract**: $BC = AC - AB$.

commonMistakes.text: Using segment addition when B isn't between A and C.
commonMistakes.correction: The postulate needs $B$ **between** $A$ and $C$ on the same line — check the picture first.

tryIt.statement: $A$ at $2$, $B$ at $8$. Where is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$?

steps.title: Parts add to the whole
steps.teach: Slide $B$ anywhere between $A$ and $C$. The two parts change, but they always add back to the same whole: $AB + BC = AC$. Snap to the midpoint and both parts match.

steps.title: The Segment Addition Postulate
beats: $B$ **between** $A$ and $C$ means all three sit on one line, $B$ inside.
Then the parts make the whole: $AB + BC = AC$.
$4 + 6 = 10$ — no gaps, no overlaps.
This is geometry's version of part + part = whole.

steps.title: Finding a missing part
beats: Know the whole and one part? Subtract.
$AC = 12$ and $AB = 5$.

steps.title: Whole and parts
steps.intro: Add the parts — or subtract from the whole.

problems.prompt: $B$ is between $A$ and $C$; $AB = 3$, $BC = 9$: $AC = $ ?
$B$ is between $A$ and $C$; $AC = 15$, $BC = 6$: $AB = $ ?
$B$ is between $A$ and $C$; $AB = 10$, $BC = 2$: $AC = $ ?

steps.title: The midpoint
beats: The **midpoint** $M$ of $\overline{AB}$ makes $AM = MB$.
Each half is $\tfrac{1}{2} AB$.
On a ruler: $m = \frac{a+b}{2}$ — the average.

steps.title: Midpoint moves
steps.intro: Halve the whole — or double the half.

steps: The half doubles back to the whole.

problems.prompt: $A$ at $2$, $B$ at $8$: the midpoint is at —
$M$ midpoint of $\overline{PQ}$, $PM = 6$: $PQ = $ ?
$B$ is between $A$ and $C$ with $AB = 7$ and $BC = 7$. What is special about $B$?
problems.explanation: The two parts are equal, so $B$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AC}$.

steps.title: Meeting in the middle
steps.body: Apps that pick a fair café for two friends do midpoint math on the map — averaging positions, exactly like $m = \frac{a+b}{2}$. Railways use it too: the meeting point of two track-laying crews is set by midpoint calculations so both sides lay the same length of rail.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $B$ between $A$ and $C$ ⇒ **$AB + BC = AC$** (Segment Addition Postulate).
Missing part = whole − known part.
**Midpoint**: equal halves, $AM = MB = \tfrac{1}{2}AB$; coordinate = average $\tfrac{a+b}{2}$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's mix wholes, parts, and midpoints — including one where you double back from a half.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: $AB = 6$, $BC = 9$, $B$ between: $AC = $ ?
$B$ is between $A$ and $C$; $AC = 20$, $AB = 8$: $BC = $ ?
$M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$, $AB = 22$: $AM = $ ?
$A$ at $1$, $B$ at $9$: the midpoint's coordinate is —
$M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$, $MB = 7$: $AB = $ ?
$AB = 10$, $BC = 4$, $B$ between $A$ and $C$. Which equation is TRUE?

steps.title: You've finished "Segment Addition & Midpoints"
steps.body: Parts, wholes, and halves — you can now solve for any of them. Next: angles! Naming & Measuring Angles brings out the protractor.

lessons.title: Naming & Measuring Angles
lessons.concreteComparison: Open a door. The hinge stays put while the door swings away from the frame — the wider the swing, the bigger the opening. That opening is an **angle**: two rays (door and frame) sharing one endpoint (the hinge), measured in degrees.
lessons.objective: Define an angle as two rays with a common vertex, name it correctly (vertex in the middle), and measure it in degrees with a protractor.
concept: An **angle** is formed by two rays with a **common endpoint**. The shared endpoint is the **vertex**; the rays are the **sides**.
Name it three ways: $\angle ABC$ (three letters, **vertex in the middle**), $\angle B$ (just the vertex, if unambiguous), or $\angle 1$ (a number in the figure).
Angles are measured in **degrees**: a full turn is $360^\circ$, so $1^\circ$ is $\tfrac{1}{360}$ of a turn. Write $m\angle ABC = 40^\circ$ for the measure.
On a **protractor**, if one side doesn't sit at $0^\circ$, read both sides and **subtract** — just like measuring a segment.
lessons.keyIdea: Angle = two rays + one vertex. Name it with the vertex in the middle; measure in degrees, subtracting protractor readings if needed.

facts.title: Measure notation
facts.explanation: The small m means 'the measure of'.

facts.title: Full turn
facts.explanation: One complete rotation; a protractor face shows half (180°).

workedExamples.statement: A protractor shows one side of an angle at $20^\circ$ and the other at $75^\circ$. Find the measure.
workedExamples.solution: Subtract the readings: $75 - 20 = 55$, so the angle measures $55^\circ$.

workedExamples.statement: In $\angle PQR$, the protractor reads $25^\circ$ for side $\overrightarrow{QP}$ and $65^\circ$ for side $\overrightarrow{QR}$. Name the vertex and find $m\angle PQR$.
workedExamples.solution: The middle letter $Q$ is the vertex ($\angle RQP$ names the same angle). Measure: $65 - 25 = 40$, so $m\angle PQR = 40^\circ$.

commonMistakes.text: Writing the vertex first or last, like ∠BAC for an angle at B.
commonMistakes.correction: The vertex letter always goes in the **middle**: the angle at $B$ is $\angle ABC$ or $\angle CBA$.
If a side isn't on $0$, the measure is the **difference** of the two readings.

tryIt.statement: Sides at $30^\circ$ and $110^\circ$ on the protractor — the angle measures?
One side sits on $0^\circ$, the other on $65^\circ$. Measure?
tryIt.solution: $65 - 0 = 65^\circ$ — with a side on zero you can read it straight off.

steps.title: Meet the protractor
steps.teach: Drag the orange ray (or step it) and read the measure live. The vertex sits at the protractor's center; one side rests on $0^\circ$; the other side's reading IS the measure.

steps.title: Two rays, one vertex
beats: Take two rays with the **same endpoint**.
The shared endpoint is the **vertex**; the rays are the **sides**.
The angle is the opening between the sides.
A wider opening = a bigger angle — the ray lengths don't matter.

steps.title: Vertex in the middle
beats: Three letters: side–vertex–side, like $\angle ABC$ — the vertex $B$ sits in the **middle**.
$\angle ABC$ and $\angle CBA$ are the **same** angle.
If only one angle lives at $B$, plain $\angle B$ works.
Figures also use numbers: $\angle 1$, $\angle 2$.

examples.prompt: Name the angle at vertex $Q$ with sides through $P$ and $R$.
Protractor readings: $20^\circ$ and $75^\circ$. Find the measure.
steps: Neither side is on zero — subtract.

problems.prompt: In $\angle XYZ$, the vertex is —
problems.explanation: The middle letter is always the vertex.

problems.prompt: Sides at $30^\circ$ and $110^\circ$: the measure is —
Which names the SAME angle as $\angle ABC$?
problems.explanation: Same vertex $B$ in the middle, sides in either order.

steps.title: How angles are counted
beats: A **full turn** is $360^\circ$.
So $1^\circ$ is $\tfrac{1}{360}$ of a turn — a very thin slice.
A protractor face shows half a turn: $0^\circ$ to $180^\circ$.
Write measures with the little $m$: $m\angle ABC = 40^\circ$.

steps.title: Reading a protractor
steps.intro: Subtract when neither side is on $0^\circ$.

examples.prompt: Sides on $45^\circ$ and $130^\circ$: find the measure.
One side on $0^\circ$, the other on $155^\circ$: measure?
steps: A side on zero: read directly.

problems.prompt: Readings $60^\circ$ and $145^\circ$: the angle is —
Readings $90^\circ$ and $25^\circ$: the angle is —

steps.title: Why 360?
steps.body: The choice of $360$ degrees comes from ancient Babylon: their number system ran on $60$s, and a year runs close to $360$ days, so the sun seems to walk about one degree along the sky each day. $360$ also divides evenly by $2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12\ldots$ — handy long before calculators.

steps.title: Read it
steps.prompt: Sides at $15^\circ$ and $70^\circ$: the measure is —

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Angle** = two rays (sides) + a common endpoint (**vertex**).
Name with the **vertex in the middle**: $\angle ABC$ = $\angle CBA$; measure written $m\angle ABC$.
Degrees count turn ($360^\circ$ = full turn); protractor readings **subtract** when neither side is on 0.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's practice naming and protractor arithmetic — including readings that don't start at zero.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: In $\angle DEF$, the vertex is —
Sides at $40^\circ$ and $125^\circ$: measure?
One side on $0^\circ$, other on $118^\circ$: measure?
Which is NOT a name for the angle at vertex $M$ with sides through $L$ and $N$?
problems.explanation: $\angle LNM$ puts $N$ in the middle — wrong vertex.

problems.prompt: A full turn is —
problems.explanation: $360^\circ$; the protractor shows half of it.

problems.prompt: Readings $35^\circ$ and $95^\circ$ — the angle is —

steps.title: You've finished "Naming & Measuring Angles"
steps.body: You can name any angle correctly and pull its measure off a protractor. Next: Classifying Angles — acute, right, obtuse, straight.

lessons.title: Classifying Angles
lessons.concreteComparison: Skaters already talk in angles: *doing a 180* means turning to face exactly backwards — a straight angle. A slice of pizza is a skinny **acute** angle; the corner of this screen is a perfect **right** angle; a laptop opened comfortably leans **obtuse**.
lessons.objective: Classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, or straight from their degree measures, with 90° and 180° as exact boundaries.
concept: **Acute**: less than $90^\circ$ — a sharp, skinny opening.
**Right**: exactly $90^\circ$ — a perfect corner, marked with a small square (□) instead of an arc.
**Obtuse**: between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$ — wider than a corner, not yet flat.

**Straight**: exactly $180^\circ$ — the two sides are opposite rays, forming a line. Notice a right angle is exactly half of this: $180 \div 2 = 90$. (Beyond $180^\circ$ an angle is called **reflex** — you'll rarely need it yet.)
lessons.keyIdea: Compare to 90 and 180: under 90 acute, exactly 90 right, between right and straight obtuse, exactly 180 straight.

facts.title: The boundaries
facts.explanation: Right and straight are EXACT values, not ranges.

facts.title: Right-angle mark
facts.explanation: A small square at the vertex means exactly 90°.

workedExamples.solution: Exactly $90^\circ$ is a right angle — half a straight angle, since $180 \div 2 = 90$.

commonMistakes.correction: $90^\circ$ is its own category: **right**. Acute stops *below* $90$; obtuse starts *above* it.

commonMistakes.text: Judging by the drawing's size on the page.
commonMistakes.correction: Classify by the **measure**, not how long the sides are drawn.

tryIt.solution: Exactly $180^\circ$: straight — the sides are opposite rays ($2 \times 90 = 180$).

steps.title: Watch the class change
steps.teach: Sweep the ray and watch the label flip exactly at $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$. The boundaries aren't fuzzy: $89^\circ$ is acute, $90^\circ$ is right, $91^\circ$ is obtuse.

steps.title: Acute, right, obtuse, straight
beats: **Acute**: $< 90^\circ$ — sharp.
**Right**: $= 90^\circ$ — a perfect corner, marked □.
**Obtuse**: between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$ — wide.
**Straight**: $= 180^\circ$ — flat; the sides are opposite rays.

steps.title: 90 and 180 are not ranges
beats: $89^\circ$? Acute. $90^\circ$? Right. $91^\circ$? Obtuse.
Right and straight are **single values**, not families.
A right angle is exactly **half** a straight one: $180 \div 2 = 90$.
That's why the square mark □ is special — it certifies exactly $90^\circ$.

problems.prompt: $47^\circ$ is —
$90^\circ$ is —
problems.explanation: Exactly $90^\circ$ — the one and only right measure ($180/2$).

problems.prompt: $155^\circ$ is —

steps.title: Straight and reflex
beats: At exactly $180^\circ$ the two sides point **opposite ways** — a straight angle looks like a line.
That connects to Lesson 2: the sides are **opposite rays**.
Past $180^\circ$? Called **reflex** — like a skater's 270.
We'll mostly live between $0^\circ$ and $180^\circ$.

examples.prompt: An angle measures exactly half of a straight angle. Classify it.

problems.prompt: $89^\circ$ is —
$180^\circ$ is —
problems.explanation: Exactly $180^\circ$ — the sides form a line.

steps.title: Angles in slang
steps.body: Skaters and snowboarders count turns in degrees: a *180* faces you backwards (straight angle), a *360* spins a full turn, and a *540* is a full turn plus a 180. When someone says a plan *did a 180*, they're doing geometry — the direction reversed exactly.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Acute** $< 90^\circ$ · **right** $= 90^\circ$ (□) · **obtuse** strictly between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$ · **straight** $= 180^\circ$.
The boundaries are **exact** — $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$ are their own classes.
A right angle is half a straight one; a straight angle's sides are opposite rays.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's classify across the whole range — including the sneaky boundary cases.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: $35^\circ$ is —
$90^\circ$ is —
$143^\circ$ is —
$180^\circ$ is —
Which measure is an acute angle just under the $90^\circ$ boundary?
A door opens from shut ($0^\circ$) to $110^\circ$. Its final opening is —

steps.title: You've finished "Classifying Angles"
steps.body: Acute to straight — you can class any measure on sight. Next: Angle Pairs, where two angles team up in four famous ways.

lessons.title: Angle Pairs
lessons.concreteComparison: Open a pair of scissors: the two blades make one angle, the two handles make another — and those two angles are **always equal**, no matter how far you open. Crossing lines can't help it. That's a **vertical pair**, the first of four angle partnerships you'll meet.
lessons.objective: Identify adjacent angles, vertical angles, linear pairs, and complementary/supplementary pairs — knowing which are congruent and which sum to 90° or 180°.
concept: **Adjacent** angles share a vertex and one side, without overlapping — neighbors.
**Vertical** angles sit across from each other where two lines cross. They are always **congruent** (equal).
A **linear pair** is two adjacent angles whose outer sides form a line (opposite rays). They are **supplementary**: they sum to $180^\circ$.
**Complementary** angles sum to $90^\circ$; **supplementary** angles sum to $180^\circ$. These are about **sums** — the two angles don't have to touch.
The big distinction: vertical pairs are **equal**; linear pairs **add to 180°**. Don't swap those.
lessons.keyIdea: Vertical = congruent. Linear pair = supplementary (180°). Complementary = 90° total. Supplementary = 180° total.

facts.title: Linear pair
facts.explanation: Adjacent, outer sides form a line.

facts.title: Complement / supplement
facts.explanation: C comes before S; 90 comes before 180.

workedExamples.statement: Two lines cross; one angle measures $115^\circ$. Find the other three.
workedExamples.solution: Vertical partner: also $115^\circ$. The two neighbors are linear pairs with it: $180 - 115 = 65^\circ$ each.

workedExamples.statement: Two angles are complementary; one is $35^\circ$. Find the other.

commonMistakes.correction: Vertical angles are **congruent** (equal). It's the *linear pair* that sums to $180^\circ$.
Alphabetical order helps: **C**omplementary ($90$) before **S**upplementary ($180$) — just as $90 < 180$.

tryIt.statement: An angle in a linear pair measures $48^\circ$. Its partner?
Two vertical angles: one is $77^\circ$. The other?

steps.title: Where two lines cross
steps.teach: Rotate the line and watch all four angles. Toggle the views: **vertical** pairs (across the X) stay equal to each other; **linear** pairs (neighbors along a line) keep summing to $180^\circ$. Both facts survive every rotation.

steps.title: Adjacent angles
beats: **Adjacent** = sharing a vertex and one side, no overlap.
Think of two pizza slices side by side.
Adjacent angles simply sit next to each other —
the special sums come when more structure appears.

steps.title: Vertical vs linear
beats: Two crossing lines make four angles.
**Across** from each other: vertical angles — always **congruent**.
**Next to** each other along a line: a linear pair — always **supplementary** ($180^\circ$).
Equal across, add-to-180 along. That's the whole grammar of a crossing.

examples.prompt: Two lines cross; $\angle 1 = 115^\circ$. Find $\angle 3$ (vertical) and $\angle 2$ (linear pair).
Complementary pair, one angle $35^\circ$: the other?

problems.prompt: Vertical to a $77^\circ$ angle is —
Linear-pair partner of $48^\circ$ is —
Complement of $62^\circ$ is —

steps.title: Inside a right angle
steps.teach: A ray inside a right angle cuts it into two pieces that always total $90^\circ$ — **complementary** angles. Move the ray: one grows exactly as the other shrinks.

examples.prompt: Find the complement and the supplement of $41^\circ$.
Two angles are supplementary and equal. Each is —

problems.prompt: Supplement of $105^\circ$?
Can a $100^\circ$ angle have a complement?
problems.explanation: A complement needs the pair to total $90^\circ$, but $100 > 90$ already.

steps.title: The scissors theorem
steps.body: Every pair of scissors is a vertical-angles machine: blades and handles always open to equal angles, which is why cutting feels symmetric. Camera tripods, folding chairs, and scaffolding braces all lean on the same fact — crossing bars lock equal angles across the joint.

steps.title: Name the pair
steps.prompt: Two angles share a side, and their outer sides form a straight line. They are —
steps.explanation: Adjacent + outer sides forming a line = linear pair (supplementary, $180^\circ$).

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Adjacent**: share vertex + side. **Vertical**: across a crossing — **congruent**.
**Linear pair**: adjacent along a line — **supplementary** ($180^\circ$).
**Complementary** sums to $90^\circ$; **supplementary** to $180^\circ$ — C before S, $90$ before $180$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's work all four partnerships — and nail the equal-vs-180 distinction that trips everyone up.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Two lines cross; one angle is $60^\circ$. Its VERTICAL partner is —
problems.explanation: Vertical angles are congruent — equal, not supplementary.

problems.prompt: Same crossing: the angle NEXT to the $60^\circ$ one (linear pair) is —
Complement of $18^\circ$?
Supplement of $18^\circ$?
Which pair is always CONGRUENT (not just summing to something)?
problems.explanation: Vertical angles are equal; the others are about sums.

problems.prompt: Two angles are complementary; one is twice the other. The smaller is —

steps.title: You've finished "Angle Pairs"
steps.body: Equal across, 180 along, 90 for complements — the four partnerships are yours. Last stop in Unit 1: Bisectors, plus your very first proof.

lessons.title: Bisectors — and Your First Proof
lessons.concreteComparison: Fold a paper corner so one edge lands exactly on the other, then unfold. The crease splits the corner into two **identical** angles — you just built an **angle bisector** with your hands. Cutting a sandwich into two equal halves does the same job for a segment.
lessons.objective: Use segment bisectors and angle bisectors to halve and double measures — and read your first two-column proof, built from Unit 1 facts.
concept: A **segment bisector** is any line, ray, or segment through the **midpoint** of a segment — it cuts the segment into two congruent halves (Lesson 4's midpoint, reused).

An **angle bisector** is a ray from the vertex that splits an angle into two **congruent** angles: if $\overrightarrow{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$, then $m\angle ABD = m\angle DBC = \tfrac{1}{2}\, m\angle ABC$.
Bisector problems run both ways: **halve** the whole, or **double** the half.
Every bisector fact can be **proved** — stated as steps, each with a reason. That's where geometry is heading next.
lessons.keyIdea: A bisector makes two congruent halves. Halve the whole ÷2; recover the whole ×2. Each claim gets a reason — that's proof.

facts.title: Angle bisector
facts.explanation: When ray BD bisects angle ABC.

facts.title: Segment bisector
facts.explanation: It passes through the midpoint M.

workedExamples.statement: $\overrightarrow{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$ and $m\angle ABC = 84^\circ$. Find $m\angle ABD$.
$\overrightarrow{QS}$ bisects $\angle PQR$ and $m\angle PQS = 33^\circ$. Find $m\angle PQR$.

commonMistakes.text: Doubling when you should halve (or the reverse).
commonMistakes.correction: Given the **whole** angle → divide by 2. Given the **half** → multiply by 2. Say which one you have first.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming any ray inside the angle bisects it.
commonMistakes.correction: A bisector needs the two parts **equal** — look for the marks or the word *bisects*.

tryIt.statement: A bisector splits a $150^\circ$ angle. Each half measures —

steps.title: The tracking bisector
steps.teach: Open or close the angle — the dashed blue ray keeps itself exactly in the middle, splitting the angle into two equal halves. The two blue arcs certify the halves match.

steps.title: Segment bisectors
beats: A **segment bisector** passes through the **midpoint**.
It splits $\overline{AB}$ into congruent halves: $AM = MB$.
Lesson 4's midpoint facts all apply.
Half = whole $\div\, 2$; whole = half $\times\, 2$.

steps.title: Angle bisectors
beats: An **angle bisector** is a ray from the vertex making two congruent angles.
$\overrightarrow{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$ ⟹ $m\angle ABD = m\angle DBC$.
Each half is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of the whole.
Figures mark the equal halves with matching arcs.

steps: Given the whole ($84^\circ$), find a half.
Given a half, recover the whole.

problems.prompt: A bisector splits $150^\circ$. Each half is —
A segment bisector crosses $\overline{AB}$ at its midpoint $M$; $AM = 8$. $AB = $ ?
$\overrightarrow{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$, $m\angle DBC = 27^\circ$. $m\angle ABC = $ ?

steps.title: Say WHY, every step
beats: You've been justifying steps all unit — quietly.
A **proof** writes it down: each **statement** paired with its **reason**. Set out in two columns — statements beside reasons — it's called a **two-column proof**.
Reasons are things we've established: *given*, *definition of midpoint*, *Segment Addition Postulate*…
Watch one built, step by step — then Unit 2 makes you fluent.

steps.title: Prove it: AB = 10
steps.teach: Every line is something you already know from this unit. That's all a proof is — familiar facts, placed in order, each with its reason.

steps.title: Reason it out
steps.intro: Match the statement to its reason.

problems.prompt: In the proof above, which reason justifies $AM = MB$?
problems.explanation: A midpoint makes the two halves equal — that's its definition.

problems.prompt: $\overrightarrow{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$ and $m\angle ABD = 42^\circ$. What does the definition of angle bisector let you conclude?

steps.title: The 2,300-year-old fold
steps.body: Euclid bisected angles with only a compass and an unmarked straightedge — no protractor, no numbers. Today's origami engineers do it with folds: crease patterns for satellite solar panels and car airbags are built almost entirely from bisected angles.

steps.title: Halve it
steps.prompt: A bisector splits a $120^\circ$ angle. Each half is —

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Segment bisector** → through the midpoint: $AM = MB$. **Angle bisector** → two congruent angles.
Halve the whole ($\div 2$); recover the whole ($\times 2$).
A **proof** = statements + reasons, each step justified by a definition, postulate, or given. Unit 2 builds on exactly this.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Last practice of Unit 1: halves, doubles, and one double-bisection finale.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A bisector splits $96^\circ$. Each half is —
$M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{PQ}$; $PM = 11$. $PQ = $ ?
$\overrightarrow{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$, with $m\angle ABD = 3x^\circ$ and $m\angle DBC = 39^\circ$. Find $x$.
In a two-column proof, every statement must come with —
problems.explanation: Statement + reason is the whole format.

problems.prompt: A $140^\circ$ angle is bisected; then one half is bisected again. The smallest angle is —
A segment bisector crosses $\overline{AB}$ at $M$ with $AM = MB = 6$. Which fact proves $AB = 12$?
problems.explanation: $AM + MB = AB$ — parts add to the whole: $6 + 6 = 12$.

steps.title: You've finished "Bisectors — and Your First Proof"
steps.body: That's Unit 1 complete: points to proofs. You've built every tool the course now runs on. Unit 2, Reasoning & Proof, turns that last page into a superpower.
